meta.title: F33 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 4 Series F33 engine — N20, N47, N55, B48, B47, B58. Real UK costs, reliability data and common failures. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 4 Series F33 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F33 engine typically costs £3,200–£7,500 supply only, depending on engine — the N20 four-cylinder petrol sits at £3,800–£6,000, while the B58 straight-six runs £4,500–£7,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 4 Series F33 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58B30 (440i, 2016–2020) — BMW's modular straight-six and arguably the most reliable engine ever fitted to a 4 Series. It's so good Toyota uses it in the Supra. With proper maintenance, it's proven to exceed 200,000 miles. The B47 diesel (post-2015 420d) is a close second.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 4 Series F33?

acceptedAnswer.text: N20 timing chain guide failure (the defining F33 petrol risk), N47 diesel timing chain stretch (early 420d), F33 retractable hardtop mechanism issues and water ingress, and — for the N55 — HPFP and oil leak problems. The B48 and B58 are significantly more reliable.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 4 Series F33 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The N20 four-cylinder petrol carries significant timing-chain risk — we recommend avoiding pre-2015 cars or budgeting for chain replacement. The N47 diesel has similar chain risks. The post-LCI B48, B47 and B58 engines are a significant reliability improvement. The convertible roof mechanism also needs regular maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 4 Series F33 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N20 four-cylinder petrol (420i/428i/430i, 2014–2017) — it has a well-documented timing-chain guide failure issue that can destroy the engine. The N47 diesel (pre-2015 420d) is a close second. If buying an F33, prioritise post-2017 LCI cars with B-series engines (B48, B47, B58).

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F33 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (N20, N47, N55), check engine light, loss of power, long cranking (HPFP issues), or blue smoke from the exhaust are the key warning signs. For the roof, listen for grinding noises or slow operation. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 4 Series F33 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £18,000 F33, a £4,500 N20 recon is ~25% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £9,000 example, the same recon brushes our 70% BMW threshold — walk away. Also factor in the condition of the convertible roof mechanism — this can be a significant additional cost.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 4 Series F33 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 straight-six can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — it's widely regarded as one of BMW's most durable engines. The B48 and B47 are similarly robust. The N20 and N47 are limited by their timing-chain design — addressed proactively, they can reach 150,000+ miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F33, F32 and F36?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the first-generation 4 Series platform. The F33 is the 2-door convertible (2014–2020, this page), the F32 is the 2-door coupé, and the F36 is the 4-door Gran Coupé liftback. The F33 has additional structural reinforcement and a heavy retractable hardtop, which adds weight and complexity — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 4 Series F33 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on N20/N47/N55/B48/B47/B58 units. Tell us your variant (420d, 420i, 428i, 430i, 435i, 440i) and year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 4 Series F33 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F33 convertible — from the N47 diesel to the N55 straight-six and the B58 that redefined the range. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the N20 timing-chain risk that still haunts early four-cylinder petrol cars. Backed by 256+ real UK F33 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 4 Series F33 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F33 convertible — from the pre‑LCI N‑series units to the post‑LCI B‑series replacements that improved reliability and performance.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: N20, N47, N55: Expert-knowledge tier, inherited from the Model Page's pattern-data-tier ratings. B48, B47: Provisional-newly-assigned tier — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet; ratings derived from specialist and owner consensus. B58: Provisional-newly-assigned tier — widely considered the most reliable engine in the F33 range, with reliability ratings derived from multiple specialist sources.

overview.intro: The F33 is the convertible body of BMW's first-generation 4 Series, launched in March 2014 as the direct successor to the E93 3 Series Convertible. It introduced a new naming convention — the 4 Series replacing the 3 Series coupé and convertible — while retaining the E93's three-piece retractable metal hardtop. The engine range spans from four-cylinder diesels and petrols to the range-topping six-cylinder N55 and B58 units. A 2017 Life Cycle Impulse (LCI) brought significant mechanical updates: the N20 four-cylinder petrol was replaced by the B48, the N55 by the B58, and the N47 diesel was superseded by the B47.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW F3x (shared with F32 Coupé, F36 Gran Coupé, F30 3 Series) • Predecessor: BMW 3 Series E93 Convertible (2007–2013) • Successor: BMW 4 Series G23 Convertible (2020–present) • Sibling body styles in this generation: F32 Coupé (2-door), F36 Gran Coupé (4-door liftback)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F33 convertible generated 256 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the N20 four-cylinder petrol and N47 diesel units as these cars age out of warranty and timing-chain issues become a significant concern.

items.quote: The B47 replaced the N47 in the 420d and brought the timing chain to the front of the engine — a significant reliability improvement. With 190 bhp and 400 Nm of torque, it delivers strong performance and genuine 50+ MPG economy. It's the diesel to have in an F33.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who need diesel economy and torque without the N47's timing-chain anxiety.

items.quote: The B58 is BMW's modular straight-six and arguably the best engine ever fitted to a 4 Series. 326–335 bhp, 450 Nm of torque, and a reputation for reliability that rivals Toyota's standards — it's so good Toyota uses it in the Supra. It transforms the F33 into a genuine grand tourer.
items.whoItsFor: Enthusiasts who want the ultimate F33 engine and are willing to pay for it.

items.quote: The N20 four-cylinder petrol suffers from timing-chain guide failure — plastic guides become brittle and break, leading to catastrophic engine failure. The chain is located at the rear of the engine, making replacement labour-intensive. Pre-2015 cars are the highest risk.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want an entry-level F33 and have budgeted for timing-chain replacement.

items.quote: When the N20's timing chain guides fail, the chain can snap, destroying the engine. A full replacement of chain, guides and tensioner costs £1,500–£2,500 at a specialist; a snapped chain means a full engine replacement at £3,800–£6,000. It's the most expensive non-catastrophic failure in the F33 range.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want to pay for preventative maintenance rather than a replacement.

items.quote: The B47-powered 420d delivers 190 bhp, 400 Nm, and 50+ MPG economy — the best all-rounder in the F33 range. It's more reliable than the N47 it replaced, more economical than the six-cylinder cars, and holds its value well. The sweet spot in the range.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want performance, economy and value in one package.

items.quote: The B48-powered 430i is often overshadowed by the 440i's six-cylinder power and the 420d's economy. But with 252 bhp and a closed-deck design that improves reliability, it's the Goldilocks engine — enough power to be exciting, enough economy to be sensible, and more reliable than the N20 it replaced.
items.whoItsFor: Buyers who want the best balance of performance, reliability and running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — N47 chain risk applies
Best Diesel — front-mounted chain, improved reliability
Highest Risk — the defining F33 petrol risk
Solid Choice — reliable, refined, closed-deck design
Best Pre-LCI Six — robust but has known issues
Best Petrol — the most reliable F33 engine

economicsRule.title: The F33 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,800–£6,000 N20 recon against a £10,000–£25,000 F33 sits at 15–60% of the car's value — a significant decision. On a sub-£10,000 high-mileage example, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £18,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 21–33% — a repair decision, not a financial one. The convertible roof mechanism also adds a unique cost dimension to F33 ownership.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F33 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 430i (B48) — reliable four-cylinder petrol, simpler than six-cylinder, no DPF.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F33 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 435i (N55) — HPFP, VANOS and oil leaks add up; six-cylinder fuel consumption.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F33 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 440i (B58) — the best engine in the range, strongest residuals, the one enthusiasts want.

problems.title: N20 Timing Chain Guide Failure
problems.rootCause: The N20 engine uses plastic timing chain guides that become brittle and crack over time. When these guides fail, the chain can stretch, skip teeth, or snap entirely. The chain is located at the rear of the engine, making inspection and replacement labour-intensive. Low engine oil levels accelerate the problem. Cold-start rattle is the classic early warning.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle or a check engine light, have the timing chain inspected immediately. A £1,500–£2,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,800–£6,000 replacement that follows if the chain snaps. On an F33 worth under £10,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N20 engine prices for your F33 420i/428i/430i →

problems.title: N47 Diesel Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The N47's rear-mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time. Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning. BMW extended the warranty on N47 timing chains to 7 years/75,000 miles, acknowledging the issue.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200+ replacement that follows if ignored. Consider a post-2015 B47-equipped 420d instead.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your F33 420d →

problems.title: F33 Retractable Hardtop Mechanism Issues
problems.rootCause: The F33's three-piece retractable metal hardtop weighs approximately 250 kg and relies on complex hydraulic and electrical systems. Common issues include water ingress into the rear compartment due to blocked drainage holes, amplifier failure from water damage, roof operation problems, and boot release failures.
problems.recommendation: Have the roof drainage holes checked and cleared regularly — a £100–£300 preventative service is dramatically cheaper than the £1,200+ repair that follows if water ingress destroys the amplifier or roof motor. Check for water sloshing noises in the rear passenger compartment.

cta.label: Compare F33 roof mechanism repair quotes →

problems.title: N55 HPFP & Oil Leak Issues
problems.rootCause: The N55's high-pressure fuel pump can fail, causing long cranking and loss of power. Oil leaks from the oil filter housing and valve cover gaskets are common. Brake vacuum pump failure is another known issue. Rod bearing failures have been reported in track use.
problems.recommendation: If your 435i has no documented HPFP history, budget for replacement as preventative maintenance — £700–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows if the engine runs lean or suffers bearing failure. Regular oil changes at 8,000-mile intervals are essential.

cta.label: Compare N55 engine prices for your F33 435i →
